description: A mid-sized songbird known for the male's brilliant red plumage and loud, clear whistle. One of the most recognizable backyard birds in eastern North America.
A small, brightly colored finch. Males are brilliant yellow in summer with black wings. Frequents weedy fields, roadsides, and gardens with seed-producing plants.
One of the most familiar North American songbirds. Recognizable by its rusty-orange breast and melodious song at dawn and dusk.
The only hummingbird breeding in eastern North America. Males have an iridescent ruby-red throat. Attracted to tubular red or orange flowers.
A stunning orange-and-black songbird of forest edges and large shade trees. Famous for its elaborate hanging nest and rich, flute-like song.
A tiny, bold bird with a black cap and bib. A year-round resident in northern North America, beloved for its cheerful chick-a-dee-dee call.
An elegant, crested bird with silky plumage, a yellow tail tip, and red waxy wingtip spots. Highly gregarious; travels in flocks to feast on berries.
A beloved symbol of spring. Males are brilliant blue above with a rusty-orange breast. Nests in cavities and is attracted to open areas with low vegetation.
A small, streaky finch originally from the American West, now widespread across North America. Males have red on the head and breast.
A highly variable sparrow with a central breast spot and melodious, complex song. Among the most widespread birds in North America.
A bright yellow warbler with rusty streaks on the breast of the male. Found in shrubby habitats near water. Feeds heavily on caterpillars.
The smallest North American woodpecker. Commonly visits suet feeders. Important for controlling wood-boring insects in native trees.
A slate-colored to variable sparrow common at feeders in winter. Shows white outer tail feathers in flight.
A perky, crested bird of eastern woodlands. A year-round visitor to feeders, known for its whistled peter-peter-peter song.
A loud, energetic wren with rich rusty-brown plumage and a bold white eyebrow stripe. Produces an impressively loud song for its small size.
Males are a stunning, deep sky-blue — one of the most brilliantly colored birds in North America. Attracted to native grasses and weedy seeds.
A compact bird that forages headfirst down tree trunks. Common at sunflower seed feeders and in mature deciduous woodland.
A slender, dark gray bird known for its cat-like mewing call and complex, rambling song. Fond of dense shrubby thickets and berry-producing shrubs.
Famous for mimicking the songs of dozens of other birds. A permanent resident of the southern U.S. Attracted to shrubs with berries.
